draft.introduction: The Bacchic gold tablets (c. 4th–3rd century BCE) were found in graves across southern Italy and Crete. Like the Orphic tablets, they serve as passports for the dead, but their language explicitly invokes Dionysus-Bacchus and the promise of release from the cycle of rebirth.

translation.text: I come pure from the pure, Queen of those below, and Eukles and Eubouleus, and the other gods and daemons; for I too claim to be of your blessed race. I have paid the penalty for deeds unrighteous. Now I come as a suppliant to holy Persephone, that she may be gracious and send me to the seats of the blessed.
translation.original_text: ἔρχομαι ἐκ καθαρῶν καθαρά, χθονίων βασίλεια, Εὔκλεε καὶ Εὐβουλεῦ καὶ θεοὶ δαίμονες ἄλλοι· καὶ γὰρ ἐγὼν ὑμῶν γένος ὄλβιον εὔχομαι

translator_notes.note: The claim “I too am of your blessed race” (ὑμῶν γένος ὄλβιον) asserts kinship with the divine. Combined with the Orphic myth that humans were formed from the ashes of the Titans who consumed Dionysus, this implies a divine fragment within mortal flesh—a doctrine strikingly parallel to Gnostic anthropology.
